topic: OpenAI Acquires PromptFoo: What It Means for AI Security Testing in 2026
keyword: OpenAI PromptFoo acquisition AI security testing

competitors.title: Promptfoo: LLM evals & red teaming
key_points: Open-source CLI/library for evaluating and red-teaming LLM applications
21,151+ GitHub stars, actively maintained since 2023
Supports testing prompts, agents, RAGs, and vulnerability scanning
Compares performance of GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama, and other models
Used by OpenAI and Anthropic for internal testing and security
Simple declarative configs with command line and CI/CD integration
Provides security vulnerability reports and red teaming capabilities
Remains open source and MIT licensed after OpenAI acquisition

competitors.title: Promptfoo Red Teaming Guide
key_points: Comprehensive red teaming framework for AI applications
Automated vulnerability scanning for prompt injection, data leakage, and jailbreaks
Generates security vulnerability reports with severity levels
Integration with GitHub Actions for automated security checks in CI/CD
Code scanning for LLM-related security and compliance issues
Battle-tested with apps serving 10M+ users in production
Developer-first approach with live reload and caching features

competitors.title: Promptfoo GitHub Action
key_points: GitHub Action for Promptfoo enables CI/CD integration
65+ GitHub stars, created June 2023
Automated testing of prompts in pull requests
Security scanning for AI applications in GitHub workflows
Part of broader AI security testing ecosystem
Demonstrates enterprise adoption patterns

competitors.title: Promptfoo Blog: AI Security Testing Insights
key_points: Regular updates on AI security best practices
Case studies of red teaming implementations
Analysis of AI vulnerabilities and attack vectors
Integration patterns with existing security tooling
Industry adoption trends for AI security testing

stats.fact: 21,151+ GitHub stars for Promptfoo (as of May 2026), indicating strong developer adoption
93% of organizations now use AI-generated code in development workflows, yet only 12% apply the same security standards to AI-generated code as traditional code
stats.source: SQ Magazine AI Coding Security Vulnerability Statistics 2026

stats.fact: 25.1% of code samples generated by AI contain a confirmed security vulnerability, based on testing 534 code samples across six major LLMs
Traditional SAST tools produce false positive rates between 30% and 70%, causing developers to distrust and ignore alerts
45% of AI-generated code contains OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities per analysis of 100+ LLMs
92% of audited AI-built applications had critical flaws, based on Sherlock Forensics assessments conducted between January and April 2026
stats.source: Sherlock Forensics AI Code Security Report 2026

stats.fact: 42% of developer-written code is now AI-assisted, projected to rise to 65% by 2027
stats.source: DryRun Security / Arnica 2026 Agentic Coding Report

stats.fact: 50 developers using AI assistants produce 75,000+ SAST findings vs 15,000 findings without AI, making traditional security review mathematically impossible
stats.source: DryRun Security Blog on AI SAST 2026

stats.fact: OWASP released Top 10 for Agentic Applications 2026 with 100+ industry experts covering Agent Goal Hijack (ASI01), Tool Misuse, and Rogue Agents (ASI10)
